text: ACHRAF. See ASHRUFF.

ACHRA-MOOCRA, the name given to a rocky barrier crossing the Essequibo in British Guayana in about 4° 16' N lat. Enormous blocks of gneiss, from 30 to 40 ft. high, and 10 to 15 ft. in diameter, here obstruct the river's course for several miles. "This rocky barrier," says Sir R. H. Schomburgk, "seems to extend some distance from the banks on either hand, in an ENE and WSW direction. The depth of water in the intervening channels was found to be from 12 to 15 fath.—a remarkable contrast to the shallowness of the river above and below this spot, which is only 2 fath. The stream rushes between the rocks at a fearful rate."—Geog. Journ., vol. vi.,

ACHRAY (Loch), a beautiful sheet of water in Perthshire, between Loch-Katrine and Loch-Vennachar. It is of small dimensions, not exceeding a mile in length, while its utmost breadth is scarcely halfa-mile; but the epithet 'lovely' has been with peculiar propriety applied to this loch by Sir Walter Scott. To quote the words of a foreigner: "The rocks are better wooded than those of Loch-Vennachar; the ridges rise and fall in beautiful variety; the prospects extend and close alternately; and the path, now rising now descending, is intersected by numerous streams and waterfalls." [Raumer.] The name in Gaelic signifies 'the Lake of the Level field.'
ACHSTETTEN, a town of Würtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, between Ulm and Biberach, on the river Nottum. Pop. 680.
ACHTERMANNSHOHE, one of the Brocken group, in the Harz chain, 3,027 ft. high.

ACHTUBA, an arm of the Volga, which branches off from its E side, 6 m. above Tzaritzin, flows in a nearly parallel course with it and seldom at a greater distance than 10 or 12 m., and falls into the Caspian, near Krasnojarsk. The greater part of its course, of nearly 300 m., is through the steppes of Astrakhan. Its banks are inhabited by wandering Tartars of the Nogay horde; and in its environs Prof. Tauscher found numerous vestiges of ancient Tartar towns.
ACHTYRKA, a town and district of Russia, in the Ukraine, 45 m. W by N of Kharkov, near the Vorskla. The environs are fertile, and there are some woollen manufactures here. Pop. 12,788.
ACHUS. See AHUS.
ACHY, a v. of France, in the arrond. of Beauvais, dep. of the Oise, on the Little Therain. Pop. 700.

ACI, or Aci-Reale, a town and commune on the E coast of Sicily, 7½ m. NE by N of Catania, in N lat. 37° 35', E long. 15° 9', at the mouth of the Acis. Distance from Palermo 138 m. It is a clean and regularly built town, founded on a mass of basaltic lava —of which substance the houses are built—at an elevation of 800 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 15,000. Point Tocco, a little to the eastward of the town, forms a mole, and what is called the marina or port of Aci. Its chief exports are wine and fruit. Flax is grown in the environs, and a considerable quantity of diapers is manufactured here.—Brydone, Smyth.

ACI-CASTELLO, a small irregularly built town on the E coast of Sicily, 4 m. NNE of Catania, on the road to Aci-Reale. Pop. 700. Like all the other towns on this coast, it is founded on immense rocks of lava which here present seawards a bold precipitous front, and are surmounted by a castle.

ACIS, a small stream on the E coast of Sicily, celebrated in classic poetry by the fable of Acis and Galatea. "It rises at once out of the earth a large stream," and rushes down with great rapidity towards the sea at Aci-Reale. Its water is remarkably pure, and so extremely cold that the stream is known by the natives as 'il Fiume freddo.' Its waters, though celebrated of old for their sweetness and salubrity, are now said to be poisonous.—Brydone.
ACKEN, or AKEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the gov. of Magdeburg, 10 m. ESE of Kalbe. Pop. 3,900.
ACKER BRUCH BERG, a ridge in the Harz mountains, SW of the Brocken. Highest alt. 2,995 ft.
ACKERMAN. See AKERMAN.
ACKLIN ISLAND, on the W side of the Mariguana passage of the Bahama group, is 42½ m. long from NNE to SSW. Highest point 200 ft. Lat. of N extremity 22° 45' N.

ACKWORTH, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area of the p. 2,270 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,828. Houses 286. The v. of Ackworth, celebrated for its school belonging to the society of Friends or Quakers, is 31 m. SSW of Pontefract.

ACLA, or AGLA, a town of Colombia, on Caledonia bay, on the W coast of the gulf of Darien, in lat. 8° 53' N, at the mouth of the Aglatomate river, and in front of Isla de Oro. It was founded in 1514, but has never risen into importance.
ACLE, a parish and market-town in Norfolk, 11 m. E from Norwich. Area 4,360 acres. Pop. in 1841, 864. Houses 177.

AC-METCHET, or AK-MESHID, the modern metropolis of the Crimea, now called Sympheropol by the Russians. It is finely situated near the centre of the peninsula, in the valley of the Salghyr, 20 m. NE of Baktschiserai, and 40 from Sevastopol, in 44° 57' N lat.; 963 m. from Moscow, and 1,408 from St. Petersburg. Both Heber and Clarke describe it as a poor place, formerly extensive, but since it fell into the hands of the Russians greatly fallen off in appearance. Kohl says that it contains, "like all the Russian new cities, many handsome houses decorated with green paint and rows of columns," and "streets of such a breadth that one can scarcely see across them." Of late years many improvements have been made upon it; and Dr. Lyall describes the cathedral as the handsomest ecclesiastical edifice he had seen in Russia. Yet a later visitor, Hommaire de Hell, laments that of the "palaces, mosques, and fine gardens," which adorned it when the residence of the Kalga sultan, "few traces now remain;" that "the tortuous streets, high walls, and rose-thickets of the old city" should have given place to the cold monotony of a Russian town; and that instead of building the new town on the banks of the river, it has been set "at the top of a great plateau." The pop., a medley of Russians, Tatars, Germans, Moldavians, Jews, Armenians, and Turks, is about 8,600. There are a Tatar seminary here and a botanic garden. A fine view of the Tchatyrdagh is obtained from this place, which from the summit of that mountain appears as if it were immediately beneath the view of the spectator. [Clarke's Travels, ii. p. 263.] The Tatar name,—which is variously written Akmetchet, Akmetchet, Akmetschet, Achmetschat,-is a corruption of Ak-Meshid, 'the White mosque,' from an edifice erected by Ibrahim Bey.
ACOBAMBA, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Ayacucho, 50 m. W by S of Huamanga. It was at one time the capital of the province. The surrounding territory is highly fertile in wheat.
ACO-COOMO, a town of Western Africa, on the SE bank of the Old Calabar river, in 6° 46' N lat. ACQ The river is here about 150 yards wide, with a powerful current.
ACO'LAH, a town and fort in the Decean, in the prov. of Berar, on the Mourna, in N lat. 20° 42'.
ACOMA, a town of New California, in N lat. 35° 24', W long. 106° 10'; 60 m. W of Albuquerque on the Rio del Norte.
ACONBURY, a parish in Hertfordshire, 4 m. S of Hertford. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 158.

ACONCAGUA, a prov. in the central jurisdiction of Chili, lying between the prov. of Coquimbo on the N, and Santiago on the S. Area 8,960 sq. m. Pop. 100,000 [Gothaischer Hof-Kalender, 1847.]. Area 4,400 sq. m. Pop. 60,000 [Encyc. Brit., 7th edn.]. It is a picturesque, well-watered and fertile territory, producing grain, vines, and olives; exporting cattle, wheat, hemp, and capsicum; and containing silver and copper mines. Its principal river is the Rio Aconcagua, which descends in a multitude of streams from the cordillera of the Andes, and the NW side of Tupungato, and after watering the fine plain of Aconcagua, in the latter part of its course, falls into the Pacific at Concon, to the N of Valparaiso, in 32° 55' S lat. Its principal tributary is the Putaenda. The highest point of the cordillera in this prov. is the summit of Aconcagua, which attains an altitude of 23,200 ft. [Fitzroy], or 23,960 ft. [Phy. Geog., ii. p. 425, 2d ed.] The principal towns are San Felipe the capital, Quillota, and Santa Rosa. The pass of Aconcagua leads between the peak of that mountain and Tupungato, across the Cumbre [alt. 12,532 ft.] to Mendoza in La Plata. The plain of Aconcagua, stretching along the lower part of the course of the river—one of the finest and most populous districts of Chili-has an elevation of about 2,600 ft. above sea-level.—Molina.—Miers.— Alcedo.—Fitzroy in Geog. Journ., vii. p. 143.

ACONQUIJA, or Anconquixa (Sierra de), a range of mountains branching-off from the Sierra Ambato range of the Andes chain, in La Plata, between the 26th and 27th southern parallels, and extending eastwards into the prov. of Tucuman. At its junction with the Ambato range it rises above the snowline, but gradually sinks as it proceeds eastwards. Up to the extreme limit of vegetation it is finely clothed in wood and verdure; and it is rich in minerals, producing gold, silver, copper, iron, tin, lead, zinc, cobalt, and alum.—Alcedo.—Capt. Andrews.

ACOOAN, one of the Aleutian isles, in the Lisii group, lying to the northward of Coogalga, and forming part of the strait of Oonemak. It is about 24 m. in length by 9 in breadth; and is surrounded by dangerous reefs. Pop. 72.

ACOONO-COONO, a large town on the Old Calabar river, in 6° 25' N. lat., in the Omun territory. It extends in a narrow stripe for about three-quarters of a mile along the eastern bank of the river, which has here a breadth of 1,600 yards, and flows between banks of an ochreous clay with a top-soil of rich alluvium. Captain Becroft estimates the pop. at 4,000, and describes them as a finer looking people in general than those of Omun.—Geog. Journ., vol. xiv., p. 272.
ACOOTAN, one of the Aleutian isles, lying 3 m. S of Acooan. Soloviof estimates its length at 80 versts. There is an active volcano on this island. Pop. 60.

ACORTIZAL, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, descending from the Serra Seiada, and running in a SE by S course towards the Parana, which it joins on the r. bank, under the 20th southern parallel.
ACOUS, or Accous, a v. of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, on the Val d'Aspe, 15 m. S of Oleron. Pop. 1,600. Cyprien Despourrins, the Burns of the French Pyrénées, was born here in 1698.
ACQUABELLA (CAPE), a low promontory on the W coast of the Adriatic, on the coast of Abruzzo Citeriore, a little to the N of Ortona.
ACQUACKANONCK, a town in Passaic co., New Jersey, 13 m. NW of New York, on the W bank and at the head of the sloop-navigation of the Passaic. Pop. 2,483.
ACQUA-LADRONE, a fishing v. at the NE extremity of Sicily, 2 m. E of Cape Rasaculmo. There is good anchorage off here.
ACQUALAGNA, a v. in the States-of-the-Church, on a hill at the junction of the Cadigliano and Cantiano, near the pass of the Furlo. Pop. 1,000.
ACQUANEGRA, or AQUANERA, a town of Lombardy, in the Cremonese, 3 m. NW of Cremona. Pop. 700.

ACQUAPENDENTE, a town and bishop's see of the Papal States, in the delegation of Orvieto, 70 m. NNW of Rome. It is picturesquely situated on a rocky and wooded eminence near the Paglia and the frontiers of Tuscany. Pop. in 1833, 3,310. A fine fall of water in the neighbourhood gives name to the town. The surrounding district, and indeed the whole route from this place to Rome, is marked by volcanic characters. The celebrated anatomist Girolamo Fabrizio, better known by his Latinized name Fabricius ab Aquapendente, was born here in 1537.
ACQUARIA, a town in the duchy and prov. of Modena, on the Panaro in the district of Frignano, 18 m. SW of Modena, celebrated for its warm springs.

ACQUA-VIVA, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Bari, 18 m. S by W of Bari. In 1796 it suffered greatly from an earthquake. Pop. 5,000. This was the birth-place of Denis de Molins and J. A. Molignano.—There is another Neapolitan town of the same name, but sometimes called Colle-di-Croce, in the prov. of Sannio, 24 m. NNE of Campobasso. Pop. 1,200.—There is also a v. of the name in the little republic of San Marino.

ACQUI, a town and bishop's see of Piedmont, in the upper division of the duchy of Montserrat, on the L bank of the Bormida, 17 m. SSW of Alessandria, and 44 m. SE of Turin, at the foot of the Monte- Stregone. Its pop., amounting to about 8,000, of whom nearly 500 are Jews, are chiefly employed in silk-manufactures. The town is celebrated for its hot sulphuretted baths. .Its duomo or cathedral is a fine building of the 12th and 13th centuries; and there are the remains of a Roman aqueduct in the neighbourhood. A., lying on the line of an important pass of the Apennines—that of Montenotte—suffered during the operations of the French generals of the republic against the Austrian forces in 1796; and was stormed by Grouchy on the 17th of March, 1799, when a popular movement in Montserrat threatened the French dominion in that quarter.

ACQUIDAH, or Acquodah, sometimes Acoda', a fort on the Guinea coast of Africa, 6 m. W of Búswa, on the eastern point, that is, the eastern headland of Cape Three Points, the S extreme of which is in N lat. 4° 45′, W long. 2° 6′.—A. was ceded by the Dutch to the Prussians towards the end of the 17th century.
ACRA. See ACCRA.
ACRAGAS. See GIRGENTI.

ACRA'TA, or AKRA'TA, a river of Achaia, which Colonel Leake identifies with the ancient Crathis. Itrises in Mount Zarúkhla, and flowing NNW receives at Klukines, on its l. bank, the Styx, descending from Mount Khelmós [7,727 ft.] - the ancient Aroania -in the middle height of which it forms a cascade of 500 ft. The A. then bends round towards NNE, and flows into the gulf of Corinth, near Cape Acráta, a bluff conspicuous point, "not exactly on the sea-side, but separated from it by a small plain covered with currant-grounds through which the river discharges itself."-Leake's Travels in the Morea, vol. iii. ACR

ACRE, a pashalik of Turkey in Asia, comprehending the ancient Phœnicia and a part of Palestine. It derives its name from its capital; and is situated between N lat. 32° 20' and 34° 30', and E long. 34° 50' and 36° 20'. It is bounded on the N by the pashalik of Tarábulus or Tripoli; on the E and S by that of Damas or Damascus, from which it is separated by the Anti-Libanus chain, and by the pashalik of Gaza; and on the W by the Mediterranean. Its greatest length, from NNE to SSW, is about 170 m.; and its average breadth is from 20 to 30 m. The harbours of Jaffa, Tour or Tyre, and Saida or Sidon, which existed on its coasts in ancient times, are now choked up; but Beirout affords a roadstead, and the bay of Akka, or St. Jean d'Acre. The highest mountains are those of the Libanus, which rise to the height of 9,350 ft. above the level of the sea. There are many extensive valleys, of which the finest is the fertile valley of Békaa; and several small rivers which pursue their course to the Mediterranean in the rainy season, but in summer are nearly all dried up. In the plains the climate is excessively hot, but tempered near the coast by the sea-breezes. The climate of the mountains is always pure, but often very cold. The soil is sandy, with a mixture, in considerable quantities, of vegetable matter and lime. There are few minerals, and only one small ironmine. Agriculture is neglected, but the fertility of the land is such that it affords rich harvests, even under the rude cultivation it obtains. The valley of Bekaa yields an extraordinary quantity of grain, and all the other products common to Asia Minor. Tobacco, lint, olives, cotton and silk, are extensively produced in this pashalik. The cultivation of silk, in particular, spreads rapidly about Beirout, and the mulberry tree flourishes abundantly all along the coast. [Bowring.] The Libanus and Anti-Libanus ranges possess thick forests of fir. The olive, fig, citron, orange, and pomegranate, form groves around the cities and hamlets; and the sugar-cane, and several kinds of aromatic plants, grow without culture. Camels, buffalos, cattle, asses, sheep, goats, poultry, and pigeons, are numerous; game is abundant; and the fisheries yield a large supply of all sorts of sea and river fish, and that particular species of shell-fish which is supposed to have produced the famous purple dye of the ancient Tyrians. The only manufactures are silk and cotton fabrics, and perfumed soap. The situation of this district is highly favourable to commerce. The exports from Acre and Beirout, its principal ports, are wool, cotton, silk, tobacco, gums, dried fruits, gallnuts, madder-root, and skins. The export trade is principally carried on with France and Italy.—According to Volney the pop. at the beginning of the present century amounted to 420,000, Turks, Greeks, Arabs, Druses, Armenians, and Maronites. This pashalik is of modern creation. In 1749 it formed part of the pashalik of Saida or Sidon, when Dháhir, son of Omar, an Arab sheikh, having invaded Acre, succeeded by his active and enterprising genius in subduing the whole pashalik to his sway, and continued in the enjoyment of it till the age of ninety, when he fell a sacrifice to the jealousy of the Porte. The Porte afterwards conferred this government on the famous Akhmed or Jezzár Pasha, who extended his rule over nearly the whole of Syria, and made Acre his capital; but his rapacity and oppression converted the fine plain of A. into a wilderness. It was subsequently usurped by Ismael Pasha, an élève of Jezzar's, on the death of the latter in 1804; but he was put to death by Suleiman, or Soleyman, an old slave of Jezzar on whom the Porte had conferred the pashalik. Suleiman was succeeded by Abdullah Pasha, whose fanaticism and cruelty were proverbial, and who managed to bring under his control the pashalik of Tripoli in addition to that of A., together with the districts of Nablous and Jerusalem. He in his turn was conquered by the enterprising pasha of Egypt, from whose grasp the whole of Syria was only rescued by the interference of Britain and Austria. While Ibrahim Pasha held Syria, the districts of Saida, Jerusalem, and Nablous, were united under one government or lieutenancy, under the governorgeneral of Syria. The revenues amounted under the old regime to 10 or 12 millions of francs, from which the pasha paid to the Porte only 750 purses, or little more than a fifth. In 1836 the revenue of Saida, which appears to have comprehended this province, was 13,726,750 piastres, or £114,000.

ACRE (SAINT-JEAN-D'), a town on the coast of Syria, the capital of the pashalik above described, situated on a low point running out into the bay of Acre, formed by the promontory of Mount Carmel [1,726 ft.] ending in Cape Carmel on the SW, and the extremity of the plain of Acre on the NE. Into the southern side of this bay flows the Kishon after skirting the eastern base of Carmel; and the Belus falls into it a little to the SE. of Acre. The town is situated in N lat. 32° 55', E. long. 35° 2½'; 4 hours from Carmel; 33 m. SSW from Tyre; 35 W from the Lake of Gennesareth; 45 from Sidon; and 65 from Jerusalem. It is called 'Akka by the Arabs; the appellation by which it is generally known in Europe was most probably derived from the Knightshospitallers of St. John. The position of Acre is advantageous for commercial intercourse with the East; but its harbour is not adapted for large vessels, being sandy and shallow, and exposed to the NW wind; nor is the climate healthy, being exposed to malaria from the stagnant pools left by the winter rains. At Hypkà or Haifá, however, below the village of Caiffa, on the south side of the bay, the anchorage is better and the water deeper. In the accompanying chart of the bay of A. the extremity of Mount Carmel is indicated by a; the village of Caiffa by b; the mouth of the Kishon by c; and that of the Belus by d. g. m. On the N and E, A. is encompassed by a fruitful plain; on the W, its walls are washed by the Mediterranean; on the S, the bay reaches to Mount Carmel. Mr. Buckingham approaching A. from Súr, says, "it is rendered interesting by the appearance of gardens and cultivated land without, and by the full foliage of innumerable trees rearing their heads within the walls." Owing to the great vicissitudes to which A., as the key of Syria, has been exposed, its pop. has been ACR is said by Knolles to have contained "50,000 able men and 40,000 of the weaker sort." The Abbé Mariti estimated it in 1760 at 16,000; Browne in 1797 at from 18,000 to 20,000; between 1832 and 1840—when it sustained its last siege—it was estimated at 22,000; and Dr. Wilson in 1843 supposed it might amount to from 8,000 to 10,000. The inhabitants consist of Mahommedans, Christians and Jews, of which the first are far the most numerous. The Jews form a small community of about 30 families.-The principal commerce of A. is in cotton and corn. The entrance of the harbour is defended by a fort rising from the sea, and a single gate gives access to the town. The city is very confined, and its streets-like those of all Turkish cities-extremely dirty and narrow. The houses are flat-roofed, and built of cut stone. "The walls are in many places double, and those on the landside are protected by strong outworks of mounds with facings of stone." [Dr. John Wilson.] It contains very few traces of antiquity, scarcely any public edifice having escaped the destructive influence of war. Amongst the few ancient buildings which still exist here are the ruins of the Castle of Iron, so called from the side towards the sea being covered with thin plates of that metal. It was the hospital and palace of the Templars. The palace of the grand-master of the knights of St. John of Jerusalem is now the residence of the pasha. In one of its towers is a great hall in the centre of which Dhahír son of Omar constructed a fountain of marble of different colours. Acre contains six mosques, of which a very beautiful one was erected by Jezzar; a Greek church, an Armenian synagogue, a small monastery of Franciscans, two bazaars or public markets, and three khans or inns for the reception of merchandise and accommodation of Europeans. The principal bath is one of the finest in the Ottoman empire. Near the palace of the pasha is a beautiful fountain of white marble taken from the ruins of ancient Cesarea. Several well-frequented cafés enliven the town. Most of the public buildings are clustered together on the NE side of the town. In the subjoined plan of the town, a marks the site of the pasha's residence; b that of the principal khan; c, Jezzar's mosque; d, the castle; e, the NW bastion; f, the SW; g, the ancient mole; h, the gate; i, the old harbour; k k, new earth-works. 03 History.] The origin of this town dates from a very remote antiquity, being alluded to under the name of Accho in the book of Judges i. 31. in a manner which implies that, even in that early period, it had acquired the power of resistance which subsequent ages so frequently evinced. From Accho the children of Israel were unable to expel the Canaanites. It was rebuilt and enlarged by Ptolemy Philadelphus B. C. 247, and from him called Ptolemais, under which name it is mentioned in the Apocrypha, Josephus, and the New Testament; but has subsequently reverted nearly to its original appellation, being called variously Acon, Akka, Acre. It was visited by St. Paul in his journey from Ephesus to Jerusalem, Acts xxi, 7. It was the principal landing-place of the Crusaders, having been captured by them in 1104. Recovered by Saladin in 1187, variously estimated at different times. In 1291 A. it was again besieged by the Christians with a force of 2,000 horse and 30,000 foot, ineffectually and with great loss for two years until reinforced by Philip Augustus of France and Richard Cœur de Lion, to whom it surrendered in 1191. The conquerors gave it to the Knights of St. John. They retained it for one century: in 1291 it yielded to the Saracens and fell into decay. "The conqueror," says Knolles, "entirely dismantled it, burnt it to the ground, digging up the very foundations of the walls, churches and other buildings, . . . and made it a fit place for husbandmen to plough and sow corn in. Thus, together with Ptolemais, the name of Christian was utterly rooted out of Syria and Palestine 192 years after the conquest of Jerusalem by Godfrey of Bouillon." After having undergone various changes in its government and condition it had so far recovered its early impregnable character, particularly under the enterprising Dhahir, that in 1799 it made a successful stand against the French army under Napoleon. On this occasion the governor, Djezzar Pasha, a man of energy and courage but of great cruelty, aided by the engineering skill of Colonel Philippeaux, a French royalist, and the counsels and intrepidity of Sir Sidney Smith, resisted the persevering efforts of Napoleon during a siege of 61 daysfrom the 18th of March to the 20th of May-when, after a reckless sacrifice of human life in eight unsuccessful assaults, he was obliged to effect a disastrous retreat into Egypt, and ultimately to relinquish his attempts at aggrandizement in that quarter. "That man," said he at St. Helena, speaking of Sir Sidney, "made me miss my destiny." In 1832 A. was taken by Ibrahim Pasha at the head of 40,000 Egyptian Arabs, after a siege of 5 months and 21 days. Though the buildings were in general destroyed, the city itself was deemed of sufficient importance to be retained as a stronghold; the fortifications were therefore repaired under the directions of the renegade Colonel Seve; but the allied powers of Europe having united to assist the sultan in his contest with his nominal vassal the pasha of Egypt, Mahomet Ali, it was once more, in 1840, bombarded and taken in a few hours by Commodore Napier. On this occasion the destruction and slaughter were fearfully augmented by the explosion of the powder magazine, by which accident 1,500 men were killed on one spot and the fortifications reduced to " a shapeless mass of smoking ruins." It is a little remarkable that a city so remote in situation from Great Britain, and so slightly connected with her, should on three distant occasions and under very different circumstances have yielded glory to the British arms, in each case under a leader of a very distinctive character. Richard Cœur de Lion was the type of chivalry of his day; Sir Sidney Smith was chivalrous to a degree bordering on romance; and in the straightforward simplicity and determined resolution of Sir C. Napier we see prominently depicted the characteristic features of a British seaman. Authorities.] Knolles' History of the Turks.—Mills' History of Chivalry. - Volney's Voyage en Syrie, tome 2. — Clarke's Travels, vol. iv. — Life of Sir Sidney Smith.—Napier's Reminiscences.—Kinnear's Travels in Syria. — Maundrell's Journey. — Buckingham's travels in Palestine.—British and Foreign Consular Reports.— Dr. Bowring's Report on Syria.—Dr. John Wilson's Lands of the Bible.

ACRI, or AGRI, a river of Continental Naples, rising in the Apennines, in Monte Maddalena, near Marsico, and flowing, with a rapid course, in a SE direction, through the prov. of Basilicata, into the gulf of Taranto, after a course of about 65 m. It is the largest river in the prov. and is identified with the ancient Aciris.
ACRI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Citeriore, in the valley of the Mucone, an affluent of the Crati, 12 m. NE of Cosenza. Pop. 7,800.

ACRO-CERAUNIAN MOUNTAINS, a broad range of mountains in Albania, now bearing the modern name Khimara, or Monti della Chimera, ranning between the districts of Illyricum and Epirus, and jutting out into the Adriatic sea in a bold promontory known as Cape Linguetta or Glóssa, which forms the NW horn of the gulf of Avlonah, and approaches to within 42 m. of the opposite coast. They intersect the modern sanjak of Avlonah from SE to NW. Their western side is rugged and precipitous, presenting a huge barrier to the waves of the Adriatic, without a port for an extent of 60 m. [Hughes], the 'infames scopulos Acrocerauniæ' on which the unfortunate bark caught by a tempest is inevitably lost. Towards the coast they descend more gradually. They are connected with the Pindus range near N lat. 40°. The Kimarriots are the most warlike and barbarous of the Arnauts, and offered a desperate resistance to the famous Ali Pasha.—Pouqueville.-Holland. * Multized by Google ACT

ACRO-CORINTHUS, a mountain of Greece, in the Peloponnesus, which anciently formed the citadel of Corinth, and was included within the walls of that city, which was built on the table-land or intermediate level which lies between its northern foot and the sea-cliffs. It is in N lat. 37° 53½ and E long. 22° 53'. Its altitude is 1,885.5 ft. above sealevel.—[Bullet. de Geogr.]—From its summit on the eastern horizon, and at a distance of 50 miles, are seen Mount Hymettus and the Parthenon. Northwards, beyond the isthmus and the bay of Lechæum, rise the Oneian range; beyond which are seen all the great summits of Locris, Phocis, Bœotia, and Attica, with the Corinthiac gulf, on the one hand, from the hill of Koryfí or Gonoessa to the dividing isthmus, and the Saronic gulf, on the other, as far as to the promontory of Sunium. Southwards the view is closed at no great distance by the mountains of Argos; and westwards it is impeded by a truncated peak which, at the distance of 1,000 yards, commands the western or lower enclosure of the fortified summit of the Acro-Corinthus.—Leake.
ACRON. See ACCRA.

ACROPOLIS, a term of frequent occurrence in Grecian topography, and literally signifying the highest part of a city; the term Acra or Acro as a prefix to Greek names implying their situation on an eminence. Thus the cities of Corinth, Messene, Argos, Sparta, Athens, and many others had their acropolis or citadel.

ACROTERI (CAPE), a high and rugged promontory forming the eastern horn of the bay of Canea on the N side of the island of Candia; in N lat. 35° 35', E long. 24° 8'. It is generally known to mariners as Cape Maleca or Melek, and may be regarded as the northern extremity of the Leucaori or White mountains.—The SW point of the island of Syra in N lat. 37° 29', E long. 24° 55', bears this name.
ACS, a town of Hungary, in the co. of Comorn, near the r. bank of the Danube, 5 m. WSW of Comorn. Pop. 3,237. It is surrounded with swamps, and suffered greatly in the civil war of 1848-49.
ACSOO. See AKSU.
ACTÆON REEF, a cluster of rocks, about a mile in length, and joined at low water, 5 m. NE by E from Point Arthur, on the SE coast of Van Diemen's Land. Lat. 43° 34' S, long. 146° 59' E.—Raper, 3d ed.
ACTÆON ISLES, a group of 3 low wooded isles in the S Pacific, 13 m. long from NW to SE. Centre in lat. 21° 23' S, long. 136° 32' W.—Geog. Journ. vii.
AC-TA'SH, a river of Russian Georgia, in Dághistán, flowing into the bay of Agrakhán, through the territory of Sheki.

ACTON, a name of frequent occurrence in English topography, and supposed to be a corruption of the Saxon Æctune, i. e. 'Oak town.' Thus we have Acton, a large p. in Cheshire. Area, 19,800 acres. Pop. in 1841, 4,134, whereof 321 were in the v. and township of Acton.—Also the township of Acton, in Weaverham p., in the same co., with a pop. of 382. -In Denbigh, in the p. of Wrexham, we have the township of Acton, with a pop. of 223.—In Middlesex there is the p. of Acton. Area 2,260 acres. Pop. 2,665. The v. of Acton in this p. is 8 m. W from St. Paul's, London.-In Northumberland, in the p. of Felton, is the v. of Acton.—In North Lydbury p., Salop, is the township of Acton, with a pop. of 108.—In Suffolk, there are a p. and a v. called Acton. Area of the p. 2,090 acres. Pop. 555.—In the W. R. of Yorkshire, in Featherston p., is the township of Acton. Pop. 76. - There are also a curacy and v. of this name in Ireland, in the p. of Ballymore, co. Armagh. Pop. of the p. in 1831, 3,843; of the v., which is 58 m. N of Dublin, 210 in 1841.—The word frequently occurs likewise as a prefix in English topography. Thus we have Acton- Beauchamp, Acton-Burnell, Acton-Grange, Acton- THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY O Pigot, Acton-Reynold, Acton-Round, Acton-Scott, Acton-Trussell, and Acton-Turville: for some of which see below.

ACTON, a town of Maine, U. S. near the headwaters of Salmon-falls river, 91 m. SW of Augusta. Pop. 1,401.—Also a town of Vermont, U. S., 18 m. NW of Brattleboro. Pop. 170.—Also a town of Massachusetts, 22 m. NW of Boston. Pop. 1,121. -Also a town of Lower Canada, on the Yamaska.
ACTON-BEAUCHAMP, a p. and v. in Worcestershire. Area of the p. 1,600 acres. Pop. in 1841, 217. The v. is 3 m. SE of Bromyard.

ACTON-BURNELL, a p. and v. in Salop. Area of the p. including the chapelry of Acton-Pigot, 2,650 acres. Pop. 394. At the v., which is <math>7\frac{1}{9}</math> m. S. of Shrewsbury, are some curious remains of the time of Edward I., in whose reign, in 1283, a parliament met here.
ACTON-SCOTT, a parish in Salop. Area 930 acres. Pop. 204.
ACTON-TURVILLE, a parish in Gloucestershire. Area 940 acres. Pop. 311.

ACTO'PAN, a town and district of Mexico, 60 m. NNE of Mexico, on the road from Itzmiquilpan to Chico, at an elevation of 7,148 ft. The valley of A. is inhabited by Otomí Indians, and in the vicinity of the town is covered with rich crops of maize and barley. The mountain-ridge known as Los Organos de Actópan, rises 2,426 ft. above the level of the plain of A. [Ward.] Pop. of the district, 12,000.
ACUL, or St. Thomas, a small seaport of Hayti, on the N coast, 8 m. SSW of Cape François now Cape Haytien.
ACULAPA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Tabasco.

ACULCO, a v. of Mexico, on the NW confines of the depart. of Mexico, about 30 m. W of Tula, near the Cazadero river. On the 7th Nov., 1810, Hidalgo was defeated by the royalist general Calléja on the plains of A. as the Llanura del Cazadero is now called. With an army of 50,000 Indians, but armed principally with bows, clubs, and slings, Hidalgo had advanced from Valladolid through the pass of Las Cruces, to within sight of Mexico, when Calleja, by a march from Querétaro, threatened to place the insurgent forces between his troops and those of the viceroy Venégas. To avoid this danger, Hidalgo commenced an ill-planned retreat, in the course of which, after six days' march, he unexpectedly encountered Callejas, who giving instant battle, without allowing his Creoles time to falter, gained a decisive victory over the insurgents.— Ward's Mexico. -Quart. Rev. vol. xxx.

ACULEO, a beautiful lake of Chili, in the prov. of Santiago, 20 m. SSW of Santiago. It is about 9 m. in length, and 3 in breadth; and discharges itself into the Rio Maypù by a small stream flowing northwards. Its scenery is said to resemble that of the Lago Maggiore in Italy.—Graham's Chile.
ACU'SHAH, a petty state of Dághistán, under the nominal authority of the shamkhal of Tarkú. Its pop., a Lesghian tribe, has been estimated at 10,000.

ACUTO (MONTE), a detached member of the Central Apennines, in the Neapolitan prov. of Capitanata. It rises about 6 m. W by N of Bovino, on the l. bank of the Cervaro.—There is a hill of this name in the States-of-the-Church about 6 m. S by E of Cesano; and another in the delegation of Frosinone, 3 m. N. of Anagni; each of which gives name to a v. in its vicinity.
ACWORTH, a town of New Hampshire, U. S., 46 m. W of Concord. Pop. 1,450.
ACY EN MULTIEN, a town of France, in the dep. of the Oise, 12 m. ESE of Senlis. Pop. 700.
ADA'. See ADDAH.
ADA, a district and v. in Abyssinia belonging to the Bayo Galla. It lies on the l. bank of the Great Inderes river, a tributary of the Jámma. Dr. Beke describes it as "a lovely, rich, level country."
ADA, a v. of Natolia, in the sanják of Kójahílí, on the N. shore of the lake of Sabanjah. It is inhabited chiefly by Armenians.
ADA (BAHR-EL), a tributary of the Bahr-el-Abiad, or White Nile, which flows in a NE direction through the country of the Donga or Dinka, and joins the Nile on its l. bank in about 10° 10' N lat.
ADABAI, a river of Abyssinia, in the district of Enori, formed by the junction of the Chácha with the Beresa in 9° 55' N lat., and 39° 22' E long. It may be regarded as the head-branch of the Jámma.
ADAGA, or ATA'KAH, the low northern point of the bay of Abu-Deraj, near the entrance of the bay of Suez. Betwixt this point and the opposite shore, the breadth is only 6 m., with a depth of 20 fath.
ADAIR (CAPE), on the W coast of Baffin's bay, lat. 71° 24' N, long. 70° 48' W.—Raper, 3d ed.

ADAIR, a central southern co. in Kentucky, U.S., watered chiefly by the Green river and its branches. Area 440 sq. m. Pop. in 1830, 8,220; in 1840, 8,466. Its capital is Columbia.—Also a co. in Missouri.
ADAIR'S HARBOUR, a good port, but with a narrow entrance, in Falkland sound; in S lat. 51° 36', W long. 59° 32'. It is sometimes known as Port Howard.

ADAJA, a river of Old Castile which rises near Villatora, 20 m. W of A'vila, and flows eastwards to that town, whence it pursues a N by E course through a richly cultivated country, to its junction with the Eresma, a little to the S of the village of Valdestillar, at about 40 m. from its source. The united stream joins the Duero on its l. bank.—Laborde.

ADA'LIA, a large and important city and sea-port of Asiatic Turkey, on the southern coast of Asia Minor, in N lat. 36° 52', and E long. 30° 45'. [Beaufort.] It is in the sanják of Tekeh, and pashalik of Anátóli. Its site is remarkably fine; and its aspect from the sea, rising from the central point of a magnificent bay, in successive terraces on the slope and to the summit of a hill, encircled by a double wall with square towers every 50 yards, and surrounded by orange-groves and gardens, is very beautiful. To the N of the city, a flat but elevated country opens a considerable distance inland. Its pop. is estimated by Capt. Beaufort at 8,000; by Messrs. Spratt and Forbes at 13,000, of whom 3,000 are Greeks, but the Turkish language is universally spoken. A. is 12 hours WSW from Kassaba, and 8 hours W from Kulla. [Keppel.] There is a caravan-road from A. to Philadelphia across the Akdagh. These travellers concur in opinion with Colonel Leake that A. is the modern representative of the ancient Attaleia founded by Attalus Philadelphus; but Captain Beaufort and Dr. Cramer, following D'Anville and Strabo, regard it as occupying the site of the ancient Olbia, and place Attaleia at Laara 5 m. to the E of the modern A. The harbour is very small; but in summer the outer road presents excellent anchorage in 15 and 20 fathoms. The Turks generally call this place Antáliyah, pronounced Andália.—Leake's Journal.—Beaufort's Karamania.—Spratt and Forbes' Travels in Lycia.

ADA'LIA (GULF OF), a spacious bay of the Mediterranean, the Pamphylian gulf of the ancients. Its western side is formed by Cape Khelidonia in E long. 30° 26', and its eastern by Cape Anamur, the southernmost point of Asia Minor, in E long. 32° 49'. Its shores are in general lofty and rugged. Off Cape Khelidonia - which is the termination of a side-branch of Mount Taurus-lie a cluster of 5 rugged islands. the Celidoni of the Italians. At 32 m. E by N from this cape is another high but rugged island called Grambusa, the Crambusa of Strabo. To the N of this, at Adratchán bay, white cliffs rise perpendicularly from the sea to the height of 600 or 700 ft., and behind them towers the majestic peak of Adratchán, the ancient Mons Phanicus. At about 4 m. N from Adratchán point, a small river, which flows round the northern foot of the mountains, enters the sea, between two rocky cliffs, at a v. called Deliktash, i. e. 'the Pierced rock,' which perhaps marks the site of the ancient Olympus. About 2 m. northwards from this v., on the side of a wooded hill, a bright and unintermitting flame issues from a volcanic aperture called Yanar, which is probably the phenomenon to which Pliny [ii. 110. c. 28] alludes when he says, "Mount Chimæra, near Phaselis, emits an unceasing flame that burns day and night." "It is nothing more," say Messrs. Spratt and Forbes, "than a stream of inflammable gas issuing from a crevice such as is seen in several places among the Apennines." The coast to the N of Deliktash is composed of a black crumbling species of rock, and clothed with fir-trees. A group of small islands, called by the Turks and Greeks, the Three islands, lies 5 m. NE by E from Deliktash; and opposite to them, about 5 m. inshore, the magnificent Takhtalu, the Mons Solyma of the ancients, and the extremity of a range running 70 m. to the northward to the main range of the Taurus, rises to the height of 7,800 ft. above sea-level. On a small peninsula at the foot of this mountain, the remains of the ancient Phaselis have been traced in the modern v. of Tekróvá. Between this and Cape Avova in N lat. 36° 35', E long. 30° 38', a belt of large pines borders the shore for several miles. From Cape Avova, a chain of mountains, the ancient Climax, extends along the shore northwards, rising in regular gradation, peak over peak, as they recede from the shore: hence their name. Alexander led in person one division of his army into the Pamphylian plain by the narrow road at the foot of these cliffs. The small uninhabited island of Rashat is separated from this bold shore by a narrow channel; and at the distance of 7 m. NE by E, lies the port of Adalia, the shore onwards to the city-described in the preceding article—being mostly a steep gravelly beach. To the E of the city extends a broad and elevated plain, terminating in abrupt cliffs which overhang the sea, "not in consequence of their base having crumbled away, but from their summit projecting in a lip which consists of parallel laminæ, each jutting out beyond its inferior layer, as if water had been continually flowing over them, and continually forming fresh accretions." Over these calcareous cliffs, between Adalia and Laara, several streams, the different mouths of the Catarrhastes, now precipitate themselves. To the E of Laara, the coast is low and sandy, and intersected by several rivers, amongst which the Cestius has a breadth at its mouth of 300 ft., and the Kópusú or ancient Eurymedon of 420 ft., but both are rendered inaccessible to boats drawing more than one foot of water by bars at their mouths. Broad, swampy, allavial plains, covered with coarse grass, here extend for some miles behind the belt of sand hills which skirt the shore; and the mountains lie far inland. A few miles beyond is Sidé, or Eski Adalia, i. e. Old Adaliah, which is in E long. 31° 25', at the mouth of the Marravgat, the ancient Melas; and to another on Sunfish creek, another 3 m. from Greenthe E of this the shore continues low and sandy to Cape Karaburnu. Further on, in E long. 31° 49', is Cape Ptolemais, whence to Alaya, a distance of 11 m. SE by E, the coast is straight and bold. At Alaya, or Alaiyeh, the barren ridge of Taurus comes down upon the shore in inaccessible cliffs of compact white limestone of from 500 to 600 ft. in height, and here begins the rugged coast of the Cilicia Aspera of low thinly wooded cape, in N lat. 46° 12', W long. 124° ADA the ancients. At Selinty, the rocky coast opens into a cultivated plain of 5 or 6 m. in breadth. Between this plain and Cape Anamur, a distance of 30 m., the rocky coast-ridge is interrupted by two ravines through which mountain-torrents find their way to the sea. The gulf of A. has been termed 'the Biscay of the Levant,' as a place of peculiarly dangerous navigation, especially in a strong SW gale. When also it is blowing hard from N or NE, the wind rushing down from the deep ravines of Mount Taurus and its offsets, from almost every point of the compass, produces something very like a whirlpool which nothing can resist.—Beaufort's Karamania.
ADALIA (OLD). See SIDE'.
ADAM (Mount), a lofty peak in Admiralty Range, on the great newly discovered Antarctic continent, in S lat. 71° 23', E long. 168° 56'.
ADAMAHO'NNI (Mount), a mountain of Tigré in Abyssinia, in the district of Eballi, 30 m. SSW of Sókota.—Beke.
ADAMELLO (MONTE), a summit of the western chain of the Tyrolese Alps, 10 m. W of Trent, in N lat. 46° 7′, E long. 10° 29′. Altitude, as ascertained by trigonometrical operation, 11,075 ft.
ADAM-KHAN-KA-MAREE, a v. in Sinde, 50 m. NE of Shikarpur, 12 m. from the l. bank of the Indus, in a low alluvial tract of country which is under water during the inundations of the river.
ADAMOWA, an unexplored district of Sudan, in about N lat. 8° 50', E long. 13° 12', through which the Shary or Chadda is supposed to flow.

ADAMPEE, a district of W Africa, on the Gold coast, lying between the Rio Volta on the E, and the Ningo river, which divides it from the Inkrau territory, on the W. On the N it is bounded by the Aquapim territory. The Danish forts of Ningo on the E, and Addah on the W, nearly mark the extent of the Adampee coast, which is throughout low; but about 8 leagues inland is a high rugged range, called the Crobo hills.

ADAMS, a county of Pennsylvania, U.S., watered by tributaries of the Susquehanna and the Potomac. Area 528 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 23,044. Capital, Gettysburg.—Also a co. of Mississippi, U. S. Area 440 sq. m. Pop. 19,434, of whom 14,241 were slaves. Capital, Natchez.—Also a co. of Ohio, U. S., on the Ohio. Area 550 sq. m. Pop. 13,183. Capital, West Union.—Also a co. of Iowa, U.S., watered by the St. Mary's and the Wabash. Area 336 sq. m. Pop. 2,264. Capital, Decatur.—Also a co. of Illinois, U.S. Area 768 sq. m. Capital, Quincy. Pop. 14,476.

ADAMS, a town of Massachusetts, U.S., 20 m. N of Pittsfield, 40 E of Albany, on the Hoosack river. Pop. 3,703.—There is a natural bridge of rock, in a body of white marble or limestone, over the Hudson's brook near this town. It is 12 ft. long, 10 ft. wide, and 60 ft. above the surface of the stream.—Saddleback mountain in the vicinity has an alt. of 3,600 ft. above sea-level.

ADAMS, a town in Jefferson co., New York, U.S., 162 m. NW of Albany, on Sandy creek. Pop. 2,966. -Also a township of Ohio, U. S., on the N side of the Tuscarawas. Pop. 838.—Also a township in the same state, 5 m. W of Cambridge. Pop. 867.—In this state are several other townships of the same name, one of which is on the Muskingum, another on Green creek, ville, another 10 m. NW of Urbanna.—The name Adams is indeed of very frequent occurrence in the topography of the U.S.; but the above list indicates the more important places bearing this favourite appellation.

ADAMS (Point), the southern point of land at the mouth of the Columbia, on the Pacific. It is a 1'. A sand-bar 5 m. in length extends from it to within a mile of Cape Disappointment on the N. side of the entrance to the Columbia, which is 7 m. distant in a NW direction. This bar has not, at the deepest part, above 4 or 41 fathoms water, and consequently generally presents a continuous line of breakers.

ADAM'S BRIDGE, a bank—called by the Hindús Ráma's bridge-which, commencing at the Wend of the island of Manár, a dependency of Ceylon, runs about 30 m. in a NW direction to the island of Ramisseram. It is about a quarter of a mile in breadth, and consists entirely of sand partly above and partly below water. On each side, at 2½ or 3 m. distance, is 6 fathoms water. There are three main openings or channels across the bank: one near Manar island, called the Tal-Manar passage; the second 8 m. to the W; and the third, 11 m. from the island of Ramisseram, termed the Tanny-Coody passage.—Major Sim's Report.
ADAM'S ISLE, or Roa Pua, one of the Marquesas, in the S Pacific. Lat. 9° 20' S, long. 140° 6' W.

ADAM'S PEAK, a lofty mountain in the interior of Ceylon, about 45 m. E by S of Colombo, in 6° 52' N lat., and 80° 29' E long., in the prov. of Saffragan, and separated from the mountains which intersect the Kandian territory by a tract of low country. The Singhalese call it Hamalell [Knox] or perhaps Hemaleh, i. e. 'the Abode of Snow.' In Sanscrit it bears the name of Samonilla. It is of a conical form, and to ships approaching the island from the westward, forms an important landmark. The road by which it is ascended is steep, and in some places dangerous, —the pilgrims frequenting it requiring the aid of iron chains fixed in the rock to drag themselves up its precipitous sides. The area on its summit is a flat oblong 72 feet in length, and 54 in breadth; and was in 1815 surrounded by a wall 5 feet high. Within this area is a mass of rock bearing the slight but distinet impress of a gigantic foot, 5 ft. 3\frac{3}{4} in. long, by 2 ft. 5 in. wide, which the Catholics maintain to be the foot-mark of St. Thomas, while the Mussulmans have a tradition that Adam, when driven out of Paradise, alighted upon this spot and left this mark while expiating his crime by standing on one foot till his sin was forgiven. [Heber.] The natives affirm it to be that of Buddha who, after 999 metamorphoses, rose from this spot to heaven; while the Hindus claim it as a memorial of their god Siva. It is surrounded by a rim of copper, ornamented by a few gems or perhaps coloured beads. A small wooden temple 12 ft. in length, 9 in width, and 41 in height, defends it from the action of the weather. [Ceylon Gazette, 1815.] The entrée to this temple is reserved for the disciples of Buddha, numbers of whom from Ceylon, Pegu, Siam, and Malacca, annually visit this sacred place. The principal rocks of the mountain are gneiss of a very fine grain, quartz, felspar, and hornblende. Its height has been greatly overestimated, having been even carried so high as 15,000 ft. In Hamilton's Gazetteer it is stated at 6,152 ft. Its perpendicular elevation, by trigonometrical measurement, appears to be 7,379 ft., which is 947 ft. less than that of Pedrotallagalla in the same range. Other observations make it 6,680 ft. The course of the Kalu-Ganga, a river which has its source at the foot of the mountain, affords the best line of route to and from it. From the v. of Ratuapoora, the road follows for a short distance the line of the Kalu-Ganga, and then suddenly ascends in so rapid a manner as to render the task to reach the peak one of no ordinary difficulty. From its summit the view is magnificent, especially westwards. -Asiatic Journal, No. 138. - Fraser's Trig. Survey.
ADAMSTHAL, a mining town of Moravia, on the Zwittawa river, 9 m. NNE of Brünn. Pop. 500. ADA Leinster. Area of the p. 8,134 acres. Pop. 2,037, chiefly Catholics.
ADAMSVILLE, the name of several small townships and villages in the United States, none of which are of sufficient importance to merit a special notice here.
ADAMUZ, a small town of Spain, in the prov. of Cordova, on the great road from Cordova to Ciudad Real, 4 m. N of the Guadalquivir. It is celebrated for its bees and honey.
ADANA'D, a town of Malabar, in the division of Shirmád, 25 m. SSE of Calicut, inhabited by a class of Brahmans called Námburis, and the place of residence of their chief, the Alvangheri Tamburakul.

ADANAH, a pashalik, sanjak, and city of Karamania. The pashalik is bounded on the N. by the Taurus range; on the E by Marash, and the Almadagh mountains which separate it from Syria; on the S by the bay of Iskenderun and the Medi terranean; and on the W by the pashalik of Itshilli, to which it had become gradually attached as a dependent district previous to its being wrested from the Porte by the victorious arms of Ibrahim Pasha in 1839. It is divided into the three sanjaks of Sis, Adanah, and Tarsús, and nearly corresponds with the ancient Cilicia campestris. The pop. consists of Anatoliotes, Turcomans, and Khurds. It is watered by the Seihun, and the Jeihun. Between these rivers extends a rich and fertile plain in which cotton and the sugar-cane are successfully cultivated. During the peninsular war the British troops drew a large proportion of their corn from this district via Malta. It supplies Syria with wool, and raises cotton to the extent of 22,000 cantars, valued at 1,000 piastres per cantar. The forests present white and yellow pines of fine growth, yellow and green straight-grained oaks, and beech of close-grained quality. Ibrahim Pasha, while in possession of the country, transported a vast quantity of fine timber from this district to Egypt. Gazelles, foxes, hares, jerboas, and ground-squirrels abound in the plains of A. The revenue collected in this pashalik in 1835 was returned along with the revenues of Syriawhich amounted to a grand total of £640,000—at 3,883,796 piastres, or £36,900, the principal branch being that of the appalti and customs, which amounted to 2,130,086 p., while the next branch, the mal miri grain and village tax, raised 788,665 p.—The city of A. rises on a gentle declivity, on the N bank of the Seihun, sometimes called the Adana, 25 m. above its mouth, in N. lat. 37°; 8 hours, or 28 m. ENE of Tarsús. It is a large and well-built place, with a pop. of nearly 20,000 [Bowring] Turks and Turcomans. On all sides it is surrounded with olivegrounds and vineyards; and beyond these extends a plain of exuberant fertility. Part of the ancient walls are yet extant; and a bridge across the riverhere 325 feet wide [Ainsworth]—is said to have been built by Justinian. The commerce of A., which was considered in 1839 the third town in Syria [Ainsworth], is considerable, and was reported by Mr. Consul Barker in 1839 to offer "a great field for speculations of every kind," though considerably impeded by the quarantine regulations, particularly those which Ibrahim Pasha had established at the Gólek-bóghaz, or pass through the Bulghár-Tágh, through which the productions of Anatolia must be transported. Mr. Ainsworth reports the Seihun to be practicable to small steamers as far as A., but its commerce is at present conducted via Tarsús and its port Mersin.—Adanah marks the site of the ancient Bathnæ. Under the Syrian kings, it was known as Antiochia ad Sarum. The bishop of A. had a seat in the councils of Nierea and Chalcedon. In 1832 it fell into the grasp of

ADAMSTOWN, a p. and v. in co. Wexford, Ibrahim Pasha, but by the treaty of 15 July 1840 it was again restored to the Porte. - Kinneir's Asia Minor.—Ainsworth in Geog. Journ. vol. x.—Bowring's Report on Syria.—Beaufort's Karamania.
ADANAQUE, or ADINAKEWY [Fraser], a fine v. of Irak-Arabi, in the pashalik of Bagdad, near the Dialak, at the foot of the Hâmrine hills.
ADAN COAST, a portion of the coast of W Africa, in about N lat. 5°, W long. 4°, sometimes known as the Quaqua coast.

ADARE, or ADAIR, a p. and t. in co. Limerick, on the Maig river. Area of the p. 11,993 acres. Pop. 4,902, chiefly Catholics. The Maig is navigable for small vessels from its confluence with the Shannon up to the town of A. which is 9 m. SSW of Limerick. The ruins of an abbey founded in 1315 exist here, and the town is remarkable for the beauty of its environs. Its pop. in 1841 was 1,095.
ADARE (CAPE), a remarkable projection of high, dark, and probably volcanic cliffs, on the coast of the newly discovered Antarctic continent, in S lat. 71° 18', E long. 170° 45'.—Sir James Ross.

ADARGA, a table-land of Abyssinia, on the N of Wogara, and to the E of Waldubba and Walkayt. It has a general elevation of about 3,000 ft. above sea-level, and its streams run in a NW direction to the Tacazzé.

ADASSI, a little square islet of the Black sea, 23 m. E from the Sulineh - boghaz or mouth of the Danube, in N lat. 43° 15', E long. 30° 10'. Its sides are bold and rocky, and it rises to nearly 100 feet above sea-level. The Greek and Russian sailors call it Fidonisi or Serpent's island.
ADA-TIPPA, a promontory running a considerable way into the lake of Sevan in Azerdbijan, and reducing the breadth, to another headland on the opposite side, to about 6 m.—Col. Monteith.
ADA-YAMAN, the largest stream falling into the lake of Sevan in Azerdbijan, near which once stood a considerable Armenian town.—Ibid.
ADBASTON, a p. and v. in Staffordshire. Area of p. 5,333 acres. Pop. 610. The v. is <math>4\frac{3}{4}</math> m. W by S from Eccleshall.

ADDA, a river of Italy, the head-streams of which, descending from the glaciers of Monte Broglio, unite near Bormio and enter the Valtellina at the defile of La Serra. In this barren and desolate region, the A. "foams from precipice to precipice in broken cataracts; lower down it shoots over a succession of natural steps which seem as if hewn by art; at the distance of about a mile it is contracted into a narrow channel through which it labours with incessant fury" [Coxe], unseen and at the depth of some hundred feet below the traveller's eye, after emerging from which it flows on in a milder stream towards Bormio. It then traverses in a course from NE to SW of about 50 m. the great valley of the Valtellina, passing Tirano on the l. and Sondrio on the r. bank; enters the lake of Como below the Laghetto, near Fuentes; emerges from its south-eastern branch near Lecco, at an alt. of 650 feet above sealevel; crosses the Milanese and the Gera d'Adda towards the Po, and joins that river on the l. bank, 7 m. above Cremona, after having passed, in a S and SE by S course, the towns of Trezzo on the r., Cassano on the 1, Lodi on the r., and Pizzighettone on the 1. bank.—It is navigable from Lodi downwards by the canal-works of the Adda and Trezzo. The canal of Trezzo, known as the Canaglia della Martessano, begins at Trezzo and is carried to Milan. It is 24 m. in length, and was first begun in 1457, but was chiefly used for the purposes of irrigation. In 1573 the cut was enlarged; and in 1619 works were begun to render the A. navigable upwards from Trezzo to Lecco. These works were completed by Joseph II. They consist of a cut about a mile in length in the | WNW of Winslow. Area 1,320 acres. Pop. 84.— ADD precipitous rock which forms the banks of the A. above Trezzo. In some places the rock has been cut to the depth of 100 ft. and a breadth of 200 ft. The breadth of the canal is 70 ft., and there are six locks. The navigation of the A. upwards is, however, of little commercial value. "The only persons," says Coxe, writing in 1787, "who forward their merchandise along the A. and the canals to the lake of Lecco are the contractors who furnish the Grisons with corn and salt, and who are compelled by the government to send those commodities by water." The A. is an impetuous and powerful stream, throughout its whole course of about 150 m., though its inundations are checked by its passage through the deep lake of Como. Of its tributaries the principal are the Ruasco, the Posciavino, the Poschiavo, the Maller, the Mallenk, and the Masino. In the long valley of the Valtellina it receives 16 tributaries, all mountain-torrents, on its l., and 15 on its r. bank. Its average breadth is about 200 feet. — The banks of the A. have witnessed many important battles. The Consul Flaminius, 223 B. C., attacked and defeated an immense host of Gauls on the plains of the A. In 490, Theodoric and Odoacer contended for the possession of Italy upon the banks of this river. During the campaign of 1796 and 1797, the banks of the A. were the scene of frequent conflicts between the Austrians and French; and its bridge at Lodi is memorable for "the terrible passage" effected by Bonaparte in 1796.
ADDAH, a Danish fort on the Gold coast of Africa, on the western bank of the Rio Volta, 11 m. from the mouth of that river, which has a depth of from 11 to 3 fathoms up to A.

ADDAH-KUDDU, or ADACADO, a walled town on the r. bank of the Niger-here called by the natives the Ujimmini Fufu, or White water—on some high rocks, 2 m. below the junction of the Chadda. It is situated on a strip of territory extending 16 m. along the river, and about 4 m. in width, which the attah of Eggarah, for 700,000 cowries, ceded to the commissioners of the Niger expedition in 1841. At its northern extremity is a table-mountain called Pattih, rising 1,200 ft. above the level of the river; and near its southern extremity, the junction of the Niger and Chadda is commanded by a hill on Barraga or Beaufort island. This cession was however subsequently declined by the British government; but one-fifth of the purchase-money was given for a piece of land within this territory to be cultivated as a model-farm. The opposite bank of the river is here low.—Parl. Paper, 1843.
ADDAR (Jebel), i. e. 'Saddle-hill,' the highest point in the range of hills to the NW of Jiddah on the Red sea.
ADDERBURY, a p. in Oxfordshire. Area 6,380 acres. Pop. 2,525. The v. of East A. is 2 m. N by E from Deddington.
ADDERGOOLE, a p. in co. Mayo, 8 m. NW by W of Foxford. Area 36,630 acres. Pop. 7,379, chiefly Catholics.
ADDERLEY, a p. in Shropshire. Area 2,260 acres. Pop. 404. It is 4 m. N of Market-Drayton.
ADDERRIG, a small p. in co. Dublin. Area 759 acres. Pop. 127.
ADDERSTONE, a township in Northumberland, 3 m. SE by S from Belford. Pop. 302.
ADDINA. See ELMINA.

ADDINGHAM, a p. of Cumberland, 6½ m. NE of Penrith. Area 9,520 acres. Pop. 735.—Also a p. and v. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. E by S from Skipton, on the Wharfe river. Area of p. 4,310 acres. Pop. 1,812.

ADDINGTON, a p. in Buckinghamshire, 2 m. Also a p. in Kent, 52 m. NW of Maidstone. Area 920 acres. Pop. 208.—Also a p. and v. in Surrey. Area of p. 3,210 acres. Pop. 580. The archbishop of Canterbury has a seat annexed to his see here. The v. is 14 m. from London, and within 2 m. of the Croydon railway.
ADDINGTON (CAPE), a headland on the W coast of Prince of Wales's island, in N. lat. 55° 27', W long. 133° 48'.
ADDINGTON (GREAT), a p. in Northamptonshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SW of Thrapston. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 266.
ADDINGTON (LITTLE), a p. in Northamptonshire, 4 m. SW of Thrapston. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 299.

ADDISCOMBE, a hamlet in the p. of Croydon, Surrey, 1 m. NE of Croydon. A military college for educating cadets for the East India company's service, has been established here. There are 14 professors and teachers connected with the college, and from 130 to 150 students.

ADDISON, a co. of Vermont, U. S., on Lake Champlain. Area 700 sq. m. Pop. 23,583. It is watered chiefly by Otter creek, which is navigable to Vergennes. Its capital is Middlebury.—Also a town of Maine, U. S., 138 m. E by N. of Augusta. Pop. 1,053.—Also a town of Vermont, U.S., 72 m. W of Montpelier. Pop. 1,232.—Also a town of New York, U. S., 227 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 1,920. -Also a town of Pennsylvania on the Youghiogheny. Pop. 1,301.—Also a town of Ohio, U. S., on the Ohio, above Gallipolis. Pop. 692.
ADDLE, a p. and v. in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 6,350 acres. Pop. 1,219. It is 4 m. NW of Leeds.
ADDLESTROP, a p. in Gloucestershire. Area 1,320 acres. Pop. 200.

ADDLETHORPE, a p. in Lincolnshire. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 238. It is 71 m. ESE of Alford. ADDOO(ATOLL), of the Maldive group, in S lat. 0° 41'. It is about 10 m. in length, and lies to the S of Suadiva atoll, the Equatorial channel between them being 46 m. broad, and presenting one small islet nearly midway called Phuwa Moloku.
ADEBAREA, a rugged sterile district of Abessinia, in the prov. of Samen, on the Lamalmon range.

ADEL, or ADA'L, a district of Eastern Africa, bounded on the N by the country of the Assabo- Gallas; on the E by the Red sea, the Straits of Babel-mandeb, and the Indian ocean; on the S by the Somauli and Harrar territories; and on the W by the country of the Galla and Angote in Abessinia. Its extent is undetermined; but though not exactly ascertained, it may be regarded as lying between the parallels of 10° 30' and 12° 30' N lat., and 42° and 43° 20' E long. This kingdom or state is sometimes called Zeila, from the sea-port of that name. It is said to have been first erected into a separate kingdom, about the beginning of the 16th cent. by Salatru, a prince of Abessinia, who, having escaped from the prison in which the royal family, according to the custom of that country, was confined, took refuge in Adel,—then an Abessinian province,—and marrying the daughter of the king of Zeila, became the independent sovereign of these united kingdoms. The kings of A. were for a long time the most formidable enemies with whom the monarchs of Abessinia had to contend. Their mutual jealousy of each other's power was heightened by the rancour of religious zeal; and the wars in which the two nations were almost perpetually engaged, were marked with every atrocity which might be expected from the rivalship and enthusiasm of savages. For their zeal in the cause of the prophet, the kings of Adel were in great favour with the Porte, to whom they were tributary. The sul- ADE tan appropriated to himself the most considerable maritime towns of the Adelians,—so that all their ports, except Zeila, were in the hands of the Turks, and are now held by the pasha of Egypt, under whom the dólah of Mokhá nominates the emir of Zeila.—The interior of A. is little known to Europeans. Mr. C. F. Rochet describes the whole country as composed of an upheaved volcanic formation, in few places susceptible of cultivation. There are a great number of extant volcanoes; but none at present in a state of activity. In some places beds of lava from 130 to 140 ft. thick are met with. Towards the SE the whole coast is a desert; from Tajurra to the kingdom of Shoa, a distance of 129 leagues, the country can be traversed only when the rains have filled the natural reservoirs met with on the road. In this tract of country M. Rochet met with numerous thermal springs whose temperature varied from 151° to the boiling-point. But in some parts of the country, the soil, composed of rich black earth washed down by torrents from Abessinia, is extremely luxuriant; and produces wheat, barley, and millet, in abundance, besides giving support to numerous flocks and herds. It seldom rains in A.; but the soil is irrigated by many rivers and canals. The climate is intensely hot, feverish, unhealthy, and generally fatal to strangers. We are informed by Hamilton and Barthema, that there is a species of sheep in A. which is entirely white, with the head of a brilliant black, and very small ears. The common sheep of A. have their neck swollen by a kind of dewlap hanging down to the ground, which demonstrates the identity of the species with the ram upon ancient marbles, and proves that this species exists in Europe, Asia, and Africa. Some of these sheep are remarkable for the size of their tails, which occasionally weigh 25 pounds; and their wool is almost as hard as the bristles of a hog. The animal kingdom here numbers various species of antelopes and gazelles, besides wild asses, ostriches, lions, panthers, hyenas, elephants, hippopotami, zebras, and an infinite number of birds. The principal articles of commerce of A. are gold-dust, ivory, frankincense, and slaves, which are exchanged for the merchandise of Arabia and the Indies. The inhabitants are chiefly nomadic tribes, whose sole occupation is tending flocks, diversified occasionally with plundering forays. They call themselves Affar. The Arabs call them Adáyil or Danákil; but they appear to have a much closer connexion with their neighbours the Gallas, than with the tribes to the N. Their most powerful tribe are the Mudaitos or Mudaitu, who occupy the tract between Aussa and Cape Beilul. Next to them are the Debenik Wéma, then the Adáli Bukharto, and Dinsarra. Their women are industrious, but slovenly in dress, frequently wearing nothing but a strip of cloth round their loins. In war they elect a ras or chief; at other times a majority of votes in the council of the tribe decides their movements. The chief town is Aussa, or Houssa, 25 leagues SSW from Tajurra. Zeila is a town of 500 souls, and chiefly composed of reed and wood houses. Tajurra, or Tajurrah, in N lat. 11° 58', has a better harbour than Zeila, but is itself a mere village. It has its own sultan, who trades with Hodeidah, Mokhá, and Aden. -M. C. F. Rochet. -MM. Isenberg and Krapf.

ADELAIDE, the capital of the province of South Australia, on the eastern side of Gulf St. Vincent, in S lat. 34° 57', E long. 138° 43'. It was founded in 1835; and is built on both banks, but chiefly on the 1. of a small stream coming from a point about 6 m. to the NE, called the Torrens, commanding a view of an extensive plain reaching down to the sea. Landward it is backed by a beautifully wooded country extending for about 6 m. to the base of the first range of hills which are capped by a high wooded one called Mount Lofty, which rises 1,200 ft. above the level of the sea. To the left, the hills gently curve round, and trend down to the coast at about 9 m. from the town, enclosing a plain country, in some places open, in others wooded, having a few small streams and freshwater lakes. To the right, the hills run in a N and E direction 30 or 40 m., and then sink into the plain. The country along their base is well-timbered; nearer the coast it is open and level. The town of A. itself has been, unfortunately perhaps, projected on a very magnificent scale. "It is," says a recent visitor, "a town large enough for the metropolis of a great nation, with about three good streets properly built upon. It is so large, as to be quite unwieldy, and, with its present population, to preclude improvement." It is laid out with perfect regalarity, the main streets being straight and parallel, and the cross ones at right angles to them. Between every two of the latter crossing the former, the intermediate space is divided into blocks of 6 or 8 double sections, each section consisting of 1 acre, in a square of about 70 yards on the side. In the centre of the incipient city are reserved 200 acres for a park; and all round it, a width of 500 yards is reserved to form a drive. There were in A. in 1847, 2 Episcopal chapels, 2 Church of Scotland chapels, 1 Roman Catholic chapel, 1 Friends' meeting-house, and 9 Dissenting chapels.—At the distance of 6 m. from the town is the head of a creek from 300 to 400 yards wide, communicating with the sea, in which vessels not drawing more than 15 ft. water may be moored safely. This creek has two openings to the gulf, the most southerly of which, in lat. 35° 47', communicates with a channel, formed by the coast and by sand-spits dry at low water, about 3 m. long, with 32, 4, and 5 fath. water, in which vessels may be moored securely, having an entrance more than 3 fath, deep at high-water spring-tides. The course of the creek is almost due N and S, running parallel to the coast for the first 4 m. from the entrance. There are 3 and 31 fath. in the middle, and 2 close to the banks; afterwards for 1 m. it deepens to 4, and then for 13 m. it becomes 5; and at the point, about 7 m. from the mouth, where it would be most convenient to form a communication between it and the capital, there is only a distance of 1 m. from the sea-shore. In the winter this creek receives the surplus waters of the Torrens; but, during the height of summer, the river-bed only presents a chain of muddy and stagnant pools. It is thought that it would be easy to dam the river above this point, and thus always keep it full; and, as the country between A. and the harbour is a dead level, it would not be an expensive work to cut a canal from the one to the other, or to construct a railway. ADE Torrens inlet; g, Mount Lofty. — The practicability of an overland journey, from Sydney to A .- a distance of 1,000 m.—has been established by Messrs. Hawdon, Eyre, and other gentlemen, conducting large flocks of cattle from the former to the latter place. The principal export of A. has hitherto been wool; but within the last four years the export of copper ores has formed a chief item in its trade. In 1845-6, no fewer than 30 mines of copper and lead were opened in S Australia. The Burra-Burra mines are distant about 86 m. N 8° E from A. The export of copper in 1847 amounted to upwards of 10,000 tons, valued at £200,000. From June 1848 to June 1849, no less than 15,598 tons of copper-ore were shipped from A. to England. Of this quantity, 13,745 belonged to the Burra-Burra company, and the remainder to seven other companies. During the same period no less than 300 tons of lead were shipped for England.—The letters passing through the general postoffice of A. in 1844 were 36,325; in 1847, 79,950.—In <math>1843,51</math> vessels <math>= 7,532</math> tons entered the port of A.; in <math>1846, 142 = 25,478 \text{ tons}</math>; in <math>1848, 215 = 46,640 \text{ tons}</math>. The county of Adelaide has an area of 1,159 sq. m., and in 1844 contained the chief portion of the pop. of South Australia, namely 14,061 souls out of a total of 17,196. It is therefore about the size of Staffordshire; and the density of its pop. was in 1844 as 42 per sq. m., or as 53 acres per head. Hindmarsh, the next most populous co. in South A., showed a density of only 1.8 per sq. m. A. county is bounded on the W by St. Vincent's gulf, and extends from Mount Terrible along the range of mountains E to Mount Barker, and N to Gawler river. In this co. agriculture is extensively prosecuted, while in the others the greater part of the pop. are engaged in pastoral pursuits. Around and within a few miles of A. are a number of thriving villages, whose inhabitants find employment in flour-mills, breweries, tanneries, smelting ores, and in agriculture and gardening. On the N and E are Hindmarsh, Bowden, Prospect, Islington, Hampstead, Walkerville, Klemzig, Payneham, Kensington, Makgill, and Woodforde. To the S and W are Goodwood, Unley, Edwardstown, Richmond, Cowandillah, and Thebartown.-A. is distant from Port Adelaide 7 m.; from Gawler Town 25 m.; from Mount Barker, 25 m.; from Morphete vale, 16 m.; from Noarlunga, 21 m.; from Willunga, 29½ m.; from Yankalilla, 40 m.; from Encounter bay, 54 m.; and from Port Lincoln, 180 m. The following meteorological table was calculated from observations made at A. during the year ending Nov. 30, 1844, between 10 A.M. and 4 P.M. of each day. Therm. Month. Days when rain fell. Quantity. Highest. Lowest. November In settled weather, the wind, throughout the whole summer, blows in land and sea-breezes, coming away from NE in the morning and veering to N, NW, W, and S, as the day advances. See South Australia.

ADELAIDE, a large river flowing northwards through Arnheim land in N Australia, the mouth of which was discovered by Capt. Wickham in 1839. It flows into Adam bay at the W entrance of Van Diemen gulf, and is navigable for 50 m. by vessels of 400 tons.

ADELAIDE ARCHIPELAGO, an extensive group of islands on the SW coast of Patagonia, near the W opening of the strait of Magalhaens. chain of islands in the Antarctic ocean, discovered by Captain Biscoe in 1832, and since called Biscoe range. A. island, of which the W point is in S lat. 67° 14′, W long. 68° 26′, has an imposing and beautiful appearance: presenting one high peak shooting up into the clouds, and occasionally appearing both above and below them, and a lower range extending about 4 m. from N to S having only a thin covering of snow on their summits, but towards their base buried in a field of snow and ice of dazzling brightness .- Geog. Journ. vol. iii.

ADELAIDE PENINSULA, a portion of the coast of Arctic America, of which the centre is nearly in N lat. 68° 10', W long. 98° 20'. It was first explored by Messrs. Dease and Simpson in 1839. Between the bottom of M'Loughlin bay on the W, and of Elliot bay on the E, it has a breadth of about 45 m. On the N, Simpson strait, leading into the gulf of Boothia, divides it from Boothia Felix.
ADELAIDE POINT, the western extremity of a small bay on the coast of Fernando Po. There is an islet off it, called Adelaide, in N lat. 3° 46', E long. 8° 47'.—Raper 3d. Ed.

ADELBODEN, a romantic Alpine valley of Switzerland, in the canton of Bern, watered by a stream of the same name which descending from the N side of the Strubel [alt. 10,978 ft.] runs NNE to join the Kander on the l. at Frutigen 43 m. from Thun. The Niesens [alt. 7,343 ft.] rise on the W side of this valley; on the E it is separated from that of the Kander by the Mittaghorn.
ADELE ISLANDS, a group off the NW coast of Australia, between York sound and Cape Levêque, in S lat. 15° 32.—Also an island of the Louisiades.
ADELEPSEN, a town of Hanover, between Göttingen and Uslar, at the foot of the Solling. Pop. 1,389, of whom 130 are Jews. A considerable quantity of linen is manufactured here.

ADELFORS, a town of Sweden, 53 m. E by S from Ionköping. A gold mine was wrought here from 1738 to 1789, when it was abandoned. The gold occurred in veins in a bed of mica slate lying in gneiss.—Thomson's Sweden.
ADELHOLZEN, or AEDLHOLZEN, a town of Bavaria, 6 m. SSW of Traunstein, celebrated for its baths.
ADELIE TERRE, a tract of land between S lat. 66° and 67°, and E long. 136° and 142°, discovered by Captain D'Urville in 1839. It presents a medium elevation of 1,300 ft.

AD-EL-JIVA'Z, a "pretty and pleasant town" of Kurdistán, on the N shore of Lake Van, 15 m. E by N of Akhlát. It contains about 250 Mahommedan and 30 Armenian families. It has an acropolis, with some ruined fortifications. A coarse cotton cloth is woven here. - Mr. Consul Brant in Geog. Journ. vol. x.
ADELMANSFELD, a manufacturing town of Würtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 6 m. W of Ellwangen. Pop. 1,137.
